message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>The study objective was to explore state death rates pre- and post- 4/19/2021 (date vaccines were assumed available) and the relative contributions of 3 factors to state death rates post- 4/19/2021: 1) vaccination rates, 2) prevalence of obesity, hypertension, diabetes, COPD, cardiovascular disease, and asthma and 3) red vs. blue states, to better understand options for reducing deaths. The ratio of red to blue state deaths/million was 1.6 pre-4/19/2021 and 2.3 between 4/19 and 2/28/2022 resulting in >222,000 extra deaths in red states or 305/ day. Adjusted betas from linear regression showed state vaccination rates had the strongest effect on death rates while red vs. blue states explained more of the difference in state death rates (60% vs. 46% for vaccination rates) with mean vaccination rates ~10% higher in blue states. Results suggest that increasing vaccination rates in red states could potentially save thousands of lives as the pandemic continues.</jats:p>
title: Implications of red state/blue state differences in COVID-19 death rates

reference.unstructured: USA Facts. US COVID-19 cases and deaths by state through date specified (April 19, 2021 or February 28, 2022) and vaccination rates as of 8/27/2021. https://usafacts.org/visualizations/coronavirus-covid-19-spread-map. Accessed 4/19/2021 and 2/28/2022.

ABC News 3/28/2022: Arielle Mitropoulos. For red and blue America, a glaring divide in COVID-19 death rates persists 2 years later. https://abcnews.go.com/Health/red-blue-america-glaring-divide-covid-19-death/story?id=83649085. Accessed March 30, 2022.

Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) (Atlanta, Georgia). Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. 2019 Survey Data and Documentation. https://www.cdc.gov/brfss/annual_data/annual_2019.html. Accessed March 31, 2022.

Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) (Atlanta, Georgia). Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System 2019 Summary Data Quality Report, July 16, 2020. https://www.cdc.gov/brfss/annual_data/2019/pdf/2019-sdqr-508.pdf, accessed March 30, 2022.
